findings.title: Installed on-the-fly via a package runner

findings.recommendation: Install the server as a pinned dependency and invoke the local binary, or vendor it. If a runner is unavoidable, pin an exact version (see unpinned_source).

findings.recommendation: Pin an exact version (pkg@1.2.3) or a git commit SHA. Avoid @latest and branch refs.

tools.name: read_file
tools.description: Read file with progressive disclosure. Default: minimal plain partial read for discovery; enable edit-ready metadata explicitly when preparing a verified edit.

candidate_capabilities.title: Filesystem read

evidence.snippet: read_file read file

candidate_capabilities.rationale: Reading file contents is the most common SOURCE half of an exfiltration chain — pair it with any network_egress tool and the server can read a secret/config/document and send it out. Modelling read as a capability is what lets the read-then-send toxic combination fire.

data_categories.label: File & document contents

evidence.snippet: read_file read file

data_categories.rationale: Documents routinely contain secrets, PII, and proprietary material.

tools.name: edit_file
tools.description: Apply hash-verified partial edits to one file. Carry base_revision on same-file follow-ups. Preserves existing line endings and trailing-newline shape; conservative conflicts return retry helpers. boundary_mode=inclusive deletes the anchor lines themselves; new_text must close any delimiter whose opening falls inside the replaced range.

evidence.snippet: edit_file edit file

candidate_capabilities.rationale: Write access lets a server plant files, overwrite configs, or stage a later code-execution path.

tools.name: outline
tools.description: AST-based structural outline with hash anchors for direct edit_file usage. Supports JavaScript/TypeScript, Python, C#, and PHP code files plus markdown headings (.md/.mdx, fence-aware).

candidate_capabilities.validation_reason: outline is read-only; it produces AST output with hash anchors for use with edit_file, but does not itself write files.
